text: The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon clears the ipsilateral retro-rectus space to expose the linea alba.
The surgeon views the pelvis and approaches the arcuate line, clearing the retroactive space and progressing towards the upper abdomen.
The surgeon begins the crossover technique using scissors, observing the absence of preperitoneal fat and noting the presence of peritoneum.
The surgeon makes an incision in the posterior rectus sheath, entering the preperitoneal space, aiming to facilitate an easier crossover in the cephalad direction.
The surgeon works back towards a previously challenging area, encountering sticky fat that reveals old mesh and V-lock sutures embedded within the tissue.

The surgeon encounters difficulty as the mesh is located in the preperitoneal space. They attempt to identify the correct surgical plane by observing reticular areolar tissue, but ultimately use the old foreign body as a guide.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the posterior rectus sheath and the hernia sac, avoiding preperitoneal fat and aiming for a bloodless field.

The surgeon uses cautery on the ventral side of the posterior rectus sheath to minimize bleeding. Scissors are then used to enter the peritoneal cavity, approaching the arcuate line below the hernia area.

The surgeon returns to the hernia area, attempting to take down the hernia sac while staying between the fat and fascia using the ETEP technique. A blue permanent V-lock suture becomes visible during the dissection.
The surgeon navigates between the visible blue V-lock sutures and the deeper tissue layers, while identifying white absorbable V-lock sutures that appear to secure the mesh circumferentially.
The surgeon inspects the peritoneum, noting the absence of bowel attachment, which informs the cautious use of cautery. The surgeon opts for more cutting than energy application when uncertain.

The surgeon navigates through a complex surgical field with visible foreign materials, including mesh and blue sutures. The dissection is carefully performed between the mesh and sutures to maintain precision.

The surgeon cuts some blue sutures to avoid deep dissection towards the peritoneum, opting for a more superficial approach as the peritoneum appears thin. Foreign material, including sutures and mesh, is visible in the area identified as a weakness, measuring approximately six by six centimeters.

The surgeon identifies blue mesh, likely Pro Grip with blue markings, situated in the preperitoneal space or preperitoneal fat. The strategy involves staying between the blue V-lock sutures through the fascia and the Pro Grip mesh.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field by closely following the contour of the blue mesh, ensuring dissection remains within the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses their left hand to press down, aiding in the dissection process to maintain close proximity to the mesh. The mesh is identified as a sizable piece of Progrip, not a smaller Ventralex.

The surgeon carefully dissects towards the patient's right side, taking precautions to avoid injuring the anterior rectus sheath. The dissection is directed slightly caudally to ensure safe progression.

The surgeon dissects below the mesh, situated below the umbilicus, entering the contralateral retroactive space. The posterior rectus sheath is visible, and the surgeon continues dissection caudally to ensure adequate space for mesh overlap, noting minimal scar tissue in the area.

The surgeon cuts the left transverse salus fascia, revealing preperitoneal fat. Subsequently, the right transverse salus fascia is incised to access the right retrorectus space. The surgeon uses their left hand as a guide to locate the linea alba, cutting between the jaws of the instrument below the arcuate line.

The surgeon identifies the contralateral retro-rectal space from the arcuate line, using it as a landmark to guide dissection in the preperitoneal space. The dissection progresses towards the posterior rectus sheath, aiming to open it without creating a hole at the junction with the peritoneum.

The surgeon navigates around a significant amount of mesh, including permanent and absorbable V-lock sutures, while carefully avoiding injury to the anterior rectus sheath. The dissection reveals the posterior rectus sheath, with a visible hint of rectus muscle on the right side, indicating a favorable dissection plane.
The surgeon carefully peels off mesh while developing the junction between the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum, ensuring no damage to the anterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon navigates a natural plane while maintaining the integrity of the peritoneum, avoiding perforations. The left hand guides the opening of the posterior rectus sheath, facilitating the dissection up to the xiphoid process. The visible six-centimeter diastasis is planned for re-approximation.

The surgeon performs contralateral retrorectus dissection near the arcuate line, navigating through areolar avascular tissue. The diastasis visibly narrows significantly by the xiphoid, and retrorectus fat is kept elevated to prevent injury to the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon dissects below the arcuate line, staying between the transversalis fascia and retrorectus fat, cutting the left and right leaflets of the transversalis fascia together. The large piece of mesh is visible on the patient's right side, and the surgeon identifies gaps in the posterior layer to be closed with V-Lock sutures.

The surgeon identifies old mesh and numerous sutures in the surgical field, focusing on reducing foreign material. The old mesh is temporarily placed in the right lower quadrant, referred to as the 'mesh graveyard.' The surgeon closes small posterior defects using a 3-0 Vicryl suture.

The surgeon closes five or six defects using figure-of-eight 3-0 Vicryl sutures. The existing mesh from previous IPOM or preperitoneal repairs serves as a bridge between the posterior rectus sheath, preventing a large posterior gap.

The surgeon removes all sutures from the surgical field and proceeds to close a larger defect near the old mesh using a Threo V-Lock 180 suture. The anterior defect, including the hernia and diastasis, is closed with an OV-Lock-I-Eighty suture, focusing on the peritoneum for healing.

The surgeon re-approximates the linea alba to bring the medial edges of the rectus muscles together, ensuring to grasp both the anterior and posterior rectus sheaths. Progressive tensioning is applied to manage the slack of the long suture, and additional bites are taken from the diastasis to reduce the seroma size.

The surgeon inserts a 20 cm wide by 34 cm long priatine mesh through an 8 mm port, unfurling it to cover the reconstructed linea alba. The mesh extends to the upper edge of the dissection, ensuring coverage of both anterior and posterior defects without the need for fixation.
